course_title: Managing Resources in Tourism, Hospitality & Events
course_description:
This course focuses on the business systems and processes for planning, organising and controlling resources in tourism, hospitality and event organisations. The course will examine the management of people, finances and risk and the implementation of sustainable and ethical business strategies. This foundation course will develop specialist theoretical and practical knowledge which will be developed further in more advanced tourism, hospitality and event management courses. The course also enhances students' employability by providing opportunities to engage in reflective practice, critical evaluation, and problem-solving in managing tourism, hospitality, and event resources for effective decision-making and communication.

This course focuses on the utilisation of business systems and processes to assist managers to plan, organise and control the available resources of tourism, hospitality and event organisations. As such, the course seeks to develop in students a knowledge of the core concepts and systems that underpin the management of human, financial and other business resources that lead toﾠthe implementation of sustainable and ethical business strategies. This foundation courseﾠthus serves as an introduction to the requisite business concepts and management principles and practicesﾠwhich are explored in greater depth in the more advanced tourism, hospitality and event management courses of the MTHEM degree program.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Assessment requirements have been further clarified by incorporating more scaffolding for assessment into the seminars.

Sustainable Development Goals - UQ Business School is a proud supporter and Advanced Signatory of the United Nations Principles for Responsible Management Education (UN PRME) . As part of the largest global collaboration between business schools and the UN, the school emphasises its role in empowering students to drive societal transformation through the Sustainable Development Goals . The SDGs highlight that a thriving economy relies on a healthy environment, aiming to balance economic growth, social well-being, and environmental protection for a sustainable future.

restrictions: Students in the MTHEM(#24) program are not permitted to enrol in this course.

course_aims: This course aims to provide students with an understanding of the myriad issues that go hand-in-hand with the ethical management of resources of any organisation operating in the global tourism, hospitality and event industry. It also seeks to advance each student's skills in collaborative and independent learning such that they are better prepared for a management career in the industry. The course enhances students' employability by providing opportunities to engage in reflective practice, critical evaluation, and problem-solving in managing tourism, hospitality, and event resources for effective decision-making and communication.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically evaluate various types of resources in tourism, hospitality and event operations.
Identify and reflect on theoretical and practical considerations associated with managing resources in tourism, hospitality and event operations.
Explain the management process and associated core business concepts related to resource management in the tourism, hospitality and event context.
Critically analyse a complex tourism, hospitality or event business problem or situation relating to resource management practices.
Formulate recommendations for strategic and ethical resource management in tourism, hospitality and event operations.

assessment_summary.title: Tourism Destination Assessment: Audit, Infographic and Panel Discussion
assessment_summary.due_date: Part A - Spreadsheet - 31/03/2026 2:00 pm; Part B - Infographic - 31/03/2026 2:00 pm; Part C - Panel Discussion - During Class - 17/04/2026

assessment_details.title: Tourism Destination Assessment: Audit, Infographic and Panel Discussion
assessment_details.due_date: Part A - Spreadsheet - 31/03/2026 2:00 pm Part B - Infographic - 31/03/2026 2:00 pm Part C - Panel Discussion - During Class - 17/04/2026
assessment_details.task_description: This is an individual three-part assessment task. You will need to complete all three components (Part A, B and C) to receive a mark for this assessment task. For this assessment task, you need to appraise a tourism destination. Prospective destinations You MUST select one of the Queensland destinations from the list provided on Blackboard. The destination will represent your client for the task. There are a limited number of representatives per destination available, allocated on a first-come, first-served basis. Once available, you must confirm your selected destination through the Blackboard sign on form. Submission Format: Part A Destination Audit Spreadsheet - 30% You are to conduct an external resource audit of the chosen destination from the listed choices and critically evaluate all the relevant natural, cultural and support resources. A spreadsheet template is available on Blackboard to guide the audit process. You have to identify relevant resources that reflect your destination characteristics . The audit must cover all resource sub-types (five natural, four cultural, four support) and include at least one resource for each type. The identified resources must reflect the destination, thus allowing for more natural or cultural resources depending on the type of destination. You are to critically evaluate each resource's appeal, quality, and uniqueness. You then identify and reflect on the main management challenges and possible strategies to overcome those challenges. Your completed spreadsheet must be submitted in Week 6 and constitutes Part A of this assessment. Completion of the spreadsheet provides the foundation for you to complete Part B. Part B Infographic - 20% Based on your research and resource audit spreadsheet, you will prepare an infographic (using Canva’s or Piktochart 's Infographic free function, or similar) from a destination marketing perspective. An infographic is a visual representation of an idea or data, and yours should be succinct, entertaining, and eye-catching. It should cite the relevant literature and incorporate informative text and engaging images or graphics to appeal and explain your findings to your audiences. ( https://www.canva.com/ ) ( https://create.piktochart.com/infographic ). Your infographic should address the following aspects: An overview of the destination (geographical location, types of attractions, visitor segment and statistics etc) A summary of the most relevant two (2) examples of natural, cultural and support resources available at this destination. This will require you to inform on each of your selected resources in terms of: i) quality, ii) uniqueness and iii) appeal (i.e. 2 x natural resources, 2 x cultural resources and 2 x support resources); and Explain the most important two (2) resource management challenges for your destination. Evidence-based justification of the identified challenges is necessary. Address each challenge strategically with one (1) possible management strategy that will overcome the challenge. The infographic therefore will include two (2) strategies, one for each of the most important challenges. Your research should be based on recent academic sources (published within the past 10 years). Attach your reference list following the APA reference guide on the audit and the infographic. Part C Destination Forum Panel Discussion Following your Audit and Infographic submission, you will participate in a Destination Forum Panel Discussion. Each panel will consist of students from different regional destinations. The panel discussion is an opportunity to demonstrate your understanding of your work by justifying your decisions and engaging with different perspectives from your peers. You will need to: Provide a brief (1 minute) verbal overview of your infographic: summarize the key resources for tourism development and key markets. Engage in a facilitated discussion with your group responding to questions from peers and the facilitator on various topics such as destination management and competitiveness, markets, management challenges and solutions. IMPORTANT: Confirmation of Assessment Integrity (Compulsory) Assessment 1 is a secure assessment task. The Destination Forum Panel Discussion (Part C) is a compulsory in-person task designed to verify that you can demonstrate you have achieved the learning outcomes for Assessment 1. You must pass Part C to be eligible to pass the Part A Audit. If you cannot demonstrate a passing standard to verify your work and meet the learning outcomes, you will receive a mark of 0 for Part A. Refer to the Assessment Task Description for further information. Please Note: The panel discussions will be recorded as per UQ Policy. Use of AI in Assessment The Destination Forum Panel Discussion (Part C) is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted in this component of the assessment. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. You may use AI to support the preparation of your Audit (Part A) and Infographic (Part B). Students using AI must disclose one AI Statement and attach an Appendix with the prompts given to AI and the results provided by AI. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Your assessment (Parts A and B) must be submitted to Turnitin, which is accessed from the Blackboard Assessments link.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Assessment 1 Part C - This part of the assessment task is NOT ELIGIBLE for discretionary extensions.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Case Study Report
assessment_details.task_description: Working INDIVIDUALLY, you are required to assess ONE of the two provided scenarios critically, identify TH&E resource management issues/problems, and provide informed consideration of the ethical implications. This assessment requires students to independently and critically evaluate a complex issue in managing THEs resources and, through problem-solving and research skills, provide a research-informed understanding of the issues and strategic, evidenced, and actionable recommendations for meaningful resolutions to the case. Through the assignment, you will need to demonstrate ethical awareness in evaluating and responding to identified issues. Prospective cases You MUST select one (1) of the case studies available on Blackboard under the Assessment 2 section. Submission Format: Prepare a clear and concise 2,500-word case study report. The word limit applies to the content and details of your report. It EXCLUDES title page, appendices, and reference list. Choose ONE scenario (selected from the two that will be made available on Blackboard) Identify the multiple key resource management issues Discuss the ethical issues associated with the main topic of the case, and any ethical issues arising from the resource management issues identified, drawing on relevant and current literature to inform the analysis Critically analyse the underlying causes and implications of the identified issues (provide evidence-based justification of your reasoning) Critically assess the consequences from a minimum of three (3) stakeholder perspectives Formulate TWO strategic, evidenced and actionable recommendations (ONE preventive and ONE reactive) to address key issues raised through problem-solving Provide a clear conclusion to the report reflecting sustainable development. Referencing: You must include at least 10 recent* and relevant academic and non-academic references following the APA7 style to support your analysis and recommendations. * Recent references are those published in the last 10 years. Use of AI in Assessment You may use AI to support the preparation of your case study report. Students using AI must disclose one AI Statement and attach an Appendix with the prompts given to AI and the results provided by AI. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
It is a course requirement that you submit your case study report via the electronic Turnitin Submission Portal available under the Blackboard Assessment link for this course. Your report should be uploaded in a Word or PDF format and it MUST be successfully submitted prior to the deadline to avoid late submission penalty. Please allow sufficient time to upload your file BEFORE the due hour and date; and ensure you secure a 'receipt' as proof of timely submission.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.topic: Introduction to Managing THEs Resources Students learn what makes tourism destinations distinct from other industries. We examine what a destination is, why effective destination management matters, who manages destinations, and how destination management processes work.

Critical Evaluation of Tourism Destinations - Natural Resource Analysis Students learn how to scope and evaluate natural resources in tourism destinations and identify key management challenges. The session includes hands-on practice to support Assessment 1.

Critical Evaluation of Destinations - Cultural and Support Resource Analysis This week focuses on assessing cultural and supporting resources and the challenges involved in managing them sustainably. Students continue hands-on practice in preparation for Assessment 1.

Destination Resource Audit Students participate in a UQ campus excursion to conduct a destination resource audit. The session supports hands-on practice for the destination audit, marketing infographic, and forum components of Assessment 1.

Dynamic Change in THEs Students explore how changes in the external environment affect organisational resources in THE. Practice analytical and problem-framing skills through a ski resort case study that directly supports Assessment 2.

Managing Human Resources in THEs Good Friday Public Holiday - Friday 3rd April 2026 - This is a Public Holiday for Good Friday so there is no face to face class. Please use BB and your online readings to explore: the evolution of human resource management in THEs and consider contemporary HR and organisational challenges such as employee engagement, effective working practices, and gender imbalance. Reminder, Part A of Assessment One is due at the beginning of this week.
In-Semester Break No classes during the mid-semester break

Destination Forum: Developing Tourism Across Queensland Representing their Assessment 1 destination, students take part in an assessed, in-class panel discussion. Active participation is required for assessment purposes.

Management, Leadership and Ethics in THEs This week distinguishes between management and leadership and examines how leadership styles influence motivation and ethical decision-making. Through role-play and activities, students experience leadership in action and reflect on their own leadership style and strengthen their applied reasoning useful for Assessment 2.

Operations & Performance Management in THEs Students practise performance analysis through a food waste journey mapping exercise. The session builds skills in identifying underlying causes and consequences of operational problems, directly supporting Assessment 2.

Technology & Innovation in THEs This week explores the role of technology and innovation in shaping the future of THE. Students creatively dream about how the sector may evolve by 2050, and practice skills for case study analysis focusing on technology challenges.

Managing Risks and Crisis in THEs Students learn the difference between hazard, risk and crisis, the multiple typologies and systemic impacts in organisational resources. Using a case study, students practise drafting clear, evidence-based recommendations (reactive and proactive) for managing risks and crises.

Climate Change Challenge for THEs Resources Students explore climate adaptation and mitigation for a future-proof industry, alongside issues of greenwashing. Additionally, we will conduct a Q&A on Assessment 2.

Bring it All Together - Resource Management for Sustainable Development Goals This week includes a discussion to consolidate learning across the course and concludes with a site visit to a Brisbane city hotel to examine a lived example of sustainability practices and certification in operations. Assessment 2 Case Study is due this week.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
